title: Simulation and Optimization of Electromagnetic Absorption of Polycarbonate/CNT Composites Using Machine Learning
ai_technique: MLP (15 specialized networks with automatic selection)
core_claim: A system of 15 specialized MLP networks with automatic selection of the appropriate network can predict the electromagnetic absorption index of polycarbonate/CNT composite films for EMI shielding, thereby replacing S-parameter measurements in the design of absorbing materials.
results: Average prediction accuracy of 99.80% with an average computation time of 0.013 s per sample; the PC composite with 5 wt.% CNT was evaluated as the best absorber in the microwave band according to the Rozanov formalism.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the system is tied to specific PC/CNT sample categories from the authors' own database, and generalizability to other material systems or wider composition ranges has not been verified.
source_file: old/29 - Simulation and Optimization of Electromagnetic Absorption of PolycarbonateCNT Composites Using Machine Learning.pdf

title: Preparation Methods for Graphene Metal and Polymer Based Composites for EMI Shielding Materials: State of the Art Review of the Conventional and Machine Learning Methods
ai_technique: Review of ML methods (mainly ANN, hybrid/ensemble models) for modeling EMI shielding composites
core_claim: A review study surveys preparation methods for graphene, metal, and polymer composites for EMI shielding and identifies a gap in the use of AI/ML methods in this field, noting that machine learning methods (especially artificial neural networks) have significantly improved EMI material modeling in recent years.
results: The alternating deposition method provides the highest shielding effectiveness (SE) for graphene and metal composites, while for polymer composites the highest SE is achieved by chemical vapor deposition (CVD); a trend toward hybrid and ensemble ML methods for achieving higher modeling performance is identified.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: this is a review article without its own experiment; the authors themselves report, as a finding, a shortage of publications combining AI/ML with the design of EMI shielding materials.
source_file: old/236 - Preparation Methods for Graphene Metal and Polymer Based Composites for EMI Shielding Materials State of the Art Review of the Conventional and Machine Learning Methods.pdf

title: Electromagnetic Data-Driven Approach to Realize the Best Microwave Absorption Characteristics of MXene-Based Nanocomposites
ai_technique: Data-driven regression model (RL prediction)
core_claim: The authors propose an electromagnetic data-driven approach that, based on a limited amount of experimental data for various weight fractions of PANI-Ti3C2Tx MXene in PVB, predicts reflection loss (RL) characteristics for any composition within the range of 0.2-59.9 wt.%.
results: The model predicted an optimal composition of 23.5 wt.% PANI-Ti3C2Tx MXene with RL = -59.5 dB in the X band (8.2-12.4 GHz) at a thickness of 1.5 mm, and the prediction was confirmed experimentally.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the model is based on a small number of experimental samples and validated for only one material system (PVB-PANI-MXene); general transferability to other composites has not been demonstrated.
source_file: old/43 - Electromagnetic Data-Driven Approach to Realize the Best Microwave Absorption Characteristics of MXene-Based Nanocomposites.pdf

title: Data mining and design of electromagnetic properties of Co/FeSi filled coatings based on genetic algorithms optimized artificial neural networks (GA-ANN)
ai_technique: Genetic algorithm-optimized artificial neural network (GA-ANN)
core_claim: The authors developed a predictive model based on a genetic algorithm-optimized artificial neural network (GA-ANN) for data mining and prediction of the electromagnetic properties of coatings filled with Co/FeSi particles at an arbitrary ratio and combination of absorbers, and subsequently used the GA to design a composition for broadband absorption.
results: The GA-ANN model exhibited lower error and higher accuracy than a conventional ANN; the optimized coating with a thickness of 2.06 mm containing 13.5 wt% Co and 55.6 wt% FeSi achieved an effective absorption bandwidth (RL below -10 dB) of 9.3 GHz, which agreed with experimental verification.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available text of the conclusion does not include a discussion of limitations; this presumably concerns the model's limitation to the studied type of absorbers (Co/FeSi) and the range of thicknesses/filling ratios used during training.
source_file: old/250 - Data mining and design of electromagnetic properties of CoFeSi filled coatings based on genetic algorithms optimized artificial neural networks (GA-ANN).pdf

title: Machine Learning to Optimize Nanocomposite Materials for Electromagnetic Interference Shielding
ai_technique: Weighted-average ensemble of 5 ML models + partial dependence/ICE analysis
core_claim: A machine learning model (a weighted-average ensemble of five base models) can rapidly predict the shielding effectiveness (EMI SE) of carbon nanocomposites from material and structural properties and reveal key design factors.
results: The resulting prediction model achieved a coefficient of determination R2 = 0.82; conductive particle filling, thickness, porosity, and number of layers were identified as the most significant factors affecting shielding effectiveness.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the model is built on a dataset compiled from published literature, whose heterogeneity and limited range of material types may limit the general transferability of the results.
source_file: old/58 - Machine learning to optimize nanocomposite materials for electromagnetic interference shielding.pdf

title: Machine Learning Empowered Magnetic Substrate Coupled Broadband and Miniaturized Frequency Selective Surface
ai_technique: Deep neural network (DNN) for inverse modeling of shielding-structure parameters
core_claim: The authors propose an FSS combined with magnetic substrates (ferrite, magnetic silicone rubber) for broadband, miniaturized EMI shielding, and use deep neural networks for the inverse design of the structure's parameters.
results: An MSE of 2.92×10⁻⁸ was achieved between the expected and predicted output; the entire X-band is shielded with a minimum SE of 10 dB and a peak SE of 56 dB at resonance (simulation), with a measured peak SE of 33 dB at 10.3 GHz; the unit-cell size is only 0.21λ × 0.21λ; angular stability up to 75°.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: a slight deviation between measured and simulated results, attributed to manufacturing and measurement inaccuracies; the DNN was trained only on data from a specific structure topology.
source_file: Machine_Learning_Empowered_Magnetic_Substrate_Coupled_Broadband_and_Miniaturized_Frequency_Selective_Surface.pdf

title: A Deep Neural Network Modeling Methodology for Efficient EMC Assessment of Shielding Enclosures Using MECA-Generated RCS Training Data
ai_technique: Deep neural network (DNN) with hyperparameter optimization (number of layers/neurons, activation, optimizer)
core_claim: The authors develop a deep neural network (DNN) methodology for predicting the radar cross section (RCS) of a shielding enclosure as a function of aperture attributes (shape, size, spacing, number), in order to efficiently evaluate the EMC performance of shielding enclosures without the need for costly full-wave simulations.
results: Based on approximately 55,000 experiments generated by the MECA method, many DNN architectures were trained and compared; the optimal DNN model showed excellent agreement with MECA predictions for previously unseen configurations at significantly lower computational cost (time and memory) than traditional numerical methods.
limitations: The authors state that future work should explore DNNs for apertures with non-uniform shapes and sizes, inverse (backward) prediction of parameters, and the susceptibility of electronics inside the enclosure to external interference sources.
source_file: old/90 - A Deep Neural Network Modeling Methodology for Efficient EMC Assessment of Shielding Enclosures Using MECA-Generated RCS Training Data.pdf

title: Machine Learning Techniques for Predicting Metamaterial Microwave Absorption Performance: A Comparison
ai_technique: Extra Trees regressor (compared against random forest, XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost, gradient boosting)
core_claim: The authors compare several machine learning regression techniques for predictive modeling and design optimization of a metamaterial microwave absorber (MMA) operating in the X and Ku bands, as an alternative to time-consuming EM simulations.
results: Among the compared regressors (decision tree, kNN, random forest, extra trees, bagging, LightGBM, XGBoost, histogram gradient boosting, CatBoost, gradient boosting), the Extra Trees regressor achieved the best results according to the adjusted R², MSE, RMSE, and MAE metrics; the proposed MMA achieves absorption of 99.42%, 98.48%, 98.92%, and 99.34% at 9.948, 13.26, 14.92, and 15.80 GHz.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: specific limitations are not given in the available text; the model's transferability is presumably limited beyond the training frequency range (X/Ku band) and the tested absorber geometry.
source_file: old/192 - Machine Learning Techniques for Predicting Metamaterial Microwave Absorption Performance A Comparison.pdf

title: Performance Optimization Engineering of Multicomponent Absorbing Materials Based on Machine Learning
ai_technique: Machine learning (regression model) combined with the extended Maxwell-Garnett model for formulation screening
core_claim: The authors propose a closed loop combining machine learning, an extended Maxwell-Garnett model, electromagnetic calculations, and experimental feedback for the rapid design of multi-component microwave absorbing materials within a practically infinite design parameter space, from very sparse data.
results: The designed NiF and NMC materials met the required absorption characteristics in the X and Ku bands at thicknesses of only 2.0 and 1.78 mm; the targets for the S band, C band, and the entire 2.0-18.0 GHz range were also achieved as expected.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the available text does not include a separate limitations section; this presumably concerns the dependence of model accuracy on the quality of the extended Maxwell-Garnett model, and limited transferability to material systems beyond the studied Ni/Fe@CF composites.
source_file: old/247 - Performance Optimization Engineering of Multicomponent Absorbing Materials Based on Machine Learning.pdf

title: Efficient EMI Analysis and Self-Shielding Design Method for Pin Map of System-in-Package
ai_technique: VBGT (generalized T-matrix) + Deep Q-Network (deep reinforcement learning)
core_claim: Combining the analytical virtual boundary generalized T-matrix (VBGT) method with deep reinforcement learning (DQN) enables automatic design of GND pin placement to suppress EMI leakage radiation from solder ball connections in system-in-package packages.
results: The VBGT method achieves a computation error below 3 dB compared to commercial software, with the computation time shortened a hundredfold; the VBGT-DRL algorithm with an online interaction module efficiently optimizes pin placement for various real configurations without requiring retraining for each new geometry.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: validation is performed on a limited number of real pin-map scenarios; the robustness of the approach for very dense or substantially different package topologies is not discussed in the text.
source_file: old/56 - Efficient EMI Analysis and Self-Shielding Design Method for Pin Map of System-in-Package.pdf

title: Optimal design of multilayer radar absorbing materials: a simulation-optimization approach
ai_technique: Simple genetic algorithm (SGA) for optimization of the impedance-graded multilayer structure
core_claim: The authors propose combining EM simulation (CST) and numerical computation (MATLAB) with substitution by a homogeneous medium and a simple genetic algorithm (SGA) for the optimal design of multilayer radar-absorbing materials (RAM) with an impedance-graded structure in the Ka-band.
results: The optimized ten-layer structure achieved a gradual impedance increase from 0 to 377 Ω and agreement between the transmission line theory calculation and the simulation; the method is described as fast and efficient compared to purely simulation-based or purely optimization-based approaches.
limitations: Not stated explicitly in the available excerpt of the conclusion; the context implies that the method is limited to structures describable by effective (homogenized) EM parameters of the individual layers, which simplifies real complex structures.
source_file: old/245 - Optimal design of multilayer radar absorbing materials a simulation-optimization approach.pdf

title: Revolutionizing Electromagnetic Materials: Machine Learning Enabled Optimization of Polymer Nanocomposites for Enhanced Performance
ai_technique: Gaussian process regression (GPR) integrated with an optimization algorithm
core_claim: The authors use Gaussian process regression (GPR) for multidimensional optimization of key parameters (filler content, thickness) of PDMS-Fe2O3 polymer nanocomposites, aiming to maximize the reflection loss (RL) and its bandwidth, in combination with an optimization algorithm operating on the predicted electromagnetic response.
results: By integrating the optimization algorithm with the GPR prediction, they achieved an optimal RLmin value of -67 dB for a 1.4 mm thick PDMS nanocomposite with 33 vol% Fe2O3, which the authors describe as the best published result to date for this type of material; experimental results confirmed the prediction.
limitations: Not stated explicitly in the available excerpt of the conclusion (it contains only the measurement methodology); this presumably concerns the GPR model's limitation to interpolation within the range of the four experimentally measured filler contents, and a single material system (PDMS-Fe2O3).
source_file: old/255 - Revolutionizing Electromagnetic Materials Machine Learning Enabled Optimization of Polymer Nanocomposites for Enhanced Performance.pdf

title: Machine Learning Approaches for Predicting Shielding Effectiveness of Carbon Fiber-Reinforced Mortars
ai_technique: Random forest, gradient boosting, GEP, AdaBoost, decision tree, kNN, SGD, stepwise regression + SHAP
core_claim: The article proposes machine learning predictive models for estimating the shielding effectiveness (SE) of carbon-fiber-reinforced cement mortars as a function of radiation frequency and mix parameters (water/cement ratio, fiber content, sand/cement ratio, fiber aspect ratio).
results: The models achieved R² values and correlation coefficients above 0.85 and 0.95, respectively, on training, testing, and validation data; the experimental/predicted SE ratio ranged from 0.5 to 1.5; SHAP analysis and GEP equations revealed a positive correlation of SE with the sand/cement ratio, fiber aspect ratio, fiber content, and frequency, and a negative correlation with the water/cement ratio.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the model is built on a limited dataset compiled from the literature, and its validity is limited to the investigated range of material parameters and frequencies.
source_file: old/79 - Machine learning approaches for predicting shielding effectiveness of carbon fiber-reinforced mortars.pdf

title: SCS-Net: A DNN-based electromagnetic shielding effectiveness analysis method for slotted composite structures
ai_technique: DNN (SCS-Net) + improved NRW equivalence, weighted L1 loss, measurement-simulation fusion (MCF)
core_claim: The SCS-Net method, based on a deep neural network, can accurately predict the electromagnetic shielding effectiveness (SE) of slotted composite structures, taking into account structural, material, and wave parameters. It addresses the shortage of measured data by fusing measurements and simulations (MCF dataset) together with an improved NRW equivalence and a weighted L1 loss function.
results: The average SE prediction error is 0.1 dB for single-frequency and 0.07 dB for swept-frequency simulations in the 8–10 GHz band; the weighted L1 norm reduces the maximum error of the equivalent EM parameters by 47% and improves accuracy on measured data by up to 23.88%, with an SE error below 0.61 dB when using the MCF dataset.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: validation is limited to the 8–10 GHz band and a small number of measured samples; the authors defer the relationships between reflection, absorption, multiple reflections, and SE to future work.
source_file: SCS-Net A DNN-based electromagnetic shielding effectiveness analysis method for slotted composite structures.pdf

title: Deep-learning approach for developing bilayered electromagnetic interference shielding composite aerogels based on multimodal data fusion neural networks
ai_technique: Multimodal neural network (FCNN + ResNet, GatedFusion)
core_claim: The authors propose a multimodal neural network fusing numerical composite-composition data with microstructural images to predict the EMI shielding effectiveness of bilayer Ag@F-ZnO/WPU/BF aerogel composites without the need for extensive experiments.
results: The best multimodal model (FCNN + ResNet with GatedFusion) achieved an RMSE of 0.7626, MAE of 0.4918, and a correlation coefficient of R=0.9885; on four new samples it predicted SE with an average error below 5%; the experimentally prepared aerogels achieved a total shielding effectiveness (SE_T) of up to 78.6 dB and an absorption coefficient of up to 0.96.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the model was trained and validated on only 16 (+4 new) samples of a single material system (Ag@F-ZnO/WPU/BF), which limits its general validity for other compositions.
source_file: Deep-learning approach for developing bilayered electromagnetic interference shielding composite aerogels based on multimodal data fusion neural networks.pdf

title: Machine Learning Surrogate Models for Electromagnetic Simulation
ai_technique: Fourier Neural Operator (FNO) and Graph Neural Network (GNN) surrogate models
core_claim: This master's thesis develops surrogate models (Fourier Neural Operator and Graph Neural Network) for the EMC Plus FDTD solver, predicting the shielding effectiveness (SE) of electrical enclosures based on geometry, material properties, and incident plane-wave illumination conditions.
results: The FNO model achieves an average GDM of 0.116-0.127 (the "excellent" range per IEEE 1597.1-2008), an MAE of ~0.03-0.035, and an inference speedup over simulation of >3000x (16 ms vs. ~50 s); the GNN model handles more complex real-world enclosure geometries with an average GDM of 0.66-0.75 (the "fair" range), is about 100x smaller than the FNO model, and provides inference in ~100 ms, though about a third of the test samples fall into the "poor" range.
limitations: The author states that for complex real-world geometries (large domains, cable bundles, enclosure seams), the FNO model becomes memory-prohibitive (>100 GiB); the GNN model has inconsistent accuracy across samples due to limited variability of geometric configurations in the training data; and both architectures may not fully capture the physics governed by Maxwell's equations. The models also do not quantify prediction uncertainty, which is a risk for safety-critical applications.
source_file: Machine_Learning_Surrogate_Mod.pdf

title: Geometric Optimization of Passive High-Frequency Electromagnetic Shielding Structures Based on Finite Element Analysis and Deep Learning
ai_technique: DBN-BP neural network (deep belief network + backpropagation) + FEA
core_claim: Geometric optimization of passive shielding for WPT systems, combining finite-element analysis with a DBN-BP neural network, enables simultaneously increasing shielding effectiveness and reducing material consumption, with a concentric-ring structure identified as the most favorable topology.
results: The DBN-BP model generates tailored parametric combinations achieving, for example, 113% SE at 71.4% material volume, or 106% SE at 43.36% material consumption, relative to the baseline design; the parametric analysis showed that SE increases with ring width and decreases with the size of the gaps between rings.
limitations: The authors state that future research must include additional real-world effects to increase model accuracy, as well as new materials; verification is simulation-only (FEA) without experimental validation.
source_file: Energy Science   Engineering - 2025 - Liu - Geometric Optimization of Passive High%E2%80%90Frequency Electromagnetic Shielding.pdf

title: A machine learning-based inverse design method for frequency-selective surface microwave absorbers
ai_technique: MLP-Mixer (inverse topology and parametric design)
core_claim: The paper proposes an ML-assisted inverse topology design method (using MLP-Mixer) for microwave FSS absorbers that maps the required electromagnetic response directly onto the topology and material parameters of a multilayer structure, thereby addressing EMC/EMI issues in electric vehicles.
results: The fabricated and measured sample achieved, at normal incidence, an absorption band of 1.33–7.31 GHz with a relative bandwidth of 138.2% at a thickness of 0.114 λL; at an incidence angle of 45°, the structure retains a wide relative bandwidth for both TE and TM polarization.
limitations: The method requires a large training dataset generated by electromagnetic simulations, which increases the initial design cost, although the inference itself is then on the order of microseconds.
source_file: A machine learning-based inverse design method for frequency-selective surface microwave absorbers.pdf

title: Design and Optimization of Wide-Band FSS Using Reinforcement Learning for X and Ku Band Radar Shielding Applications
ai_technique: Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reinforcement learning + equivalent circuit model
core_claim: A reinforcement learning framework with the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) algorithm can automatically design a complex single-layer broadband FSS for shielding radar X- and Ku-bands, outperforming conventional iterative design procedures in both accuracy and speed.
results: The designed FSS achieves a stopband of 10.42 GHz (7.88–18.30 GHz), an SE of 68 dB, and angular stability up to 60°; the SAC model (500 epochs, 1800 samples) achieved an MSE of 0.2341 versus 0.2790 for DDPG, shortened the design process by 98.73%, and predicts dimensions within 120 s; the results were verified by prototype measurement.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the approach is demonstrated on a single FSS topology and requires a dataset of 1800 simulated samples; the transferability of the RL agent to other structures and frequency bands has not been verified.
source_file: Design_and_Optimization_of_Wide-Band_FSS_Using_Reinforcement_Learning_for_X_and_Ku_Band_Radar_Shielding_Applications.pdf

title: EMI Shielding With Anisotropic Frequency Selective Surfaces: A Neural Network and Equivalent Circuit Approach
ai_technique: MLP/DNN (inverse modeling) + equivalent circuit model
core_claim: An inverse model based on a multilayer perceptron (MLP) combined with an equivalent circuit model can predict the dimensions of a coupled ring anisotropic FSS (CRAFSS) for broadband EMI shielding from the required S-parameters, resonant frequency, and shielding effectiveness.
results: The best network configuration achieved an MSE of 1.012×10⁻⁴; the designed single-layer FSS provides broadband shielding in the 6–16 GHz band with stability for incidence angles of 0–60° in both TE and TM polarization; measurements of the fabricated prototype agree well with the simulations.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors leave behavior under circular polarization, tunable materials, and multilayer structures to future research; the training data originate exclusively from simulations, and the measured SE is slightly lower than the simulated SE.
source_file: EMI_Shielding_With_Anisotropic_Frequency_Selective_Surfaces_A_Neural_Network_and_Equivalent_Circuit_Approach.pdf

title: A Machine Learning Approach for Design of Frequency Selective Surface based Radar Absorbing Material via Image Prediction
ai_technique: Random Forest / KNN / Decision Tree (classification and regression for unit-cell image prediction)
core_claim: The authors propose an inverse ML approach in which the unit-cell geometry image (Jerusalem cross) is predicted directly from the required absorption coefficient of an FSS-based radar absorbing material (RAM), thereby bypassing the need for iterative EM simulations.
results: Of the ten ML models tested, five (Random Forest classification, K-Neighbors classification, Random Forest regression, Decision Tree classification and regression) achieved training accuracy/R2 above 90%; the best was the Random Forest classifier with a training accuracy of 0.99 and a test accuracy of 0.90.
limitations: The dataset comprises only approximately 460 samples limited to a single unit-cell topology (Jerusalem cross) and a single sample thickness; the authors plan to extend this to other cell shapes and deeper models in future work.
source_file: A_Machine_Learning_Approach_for_Design_of_Frequency_Selective_Surface_based_Radar_Absorbing_Material_via_Image_Prediction.pdf

title: Design of composite reactive resonance shielding coil for EV-WPT system based on hybrid robust optimization method
ai_technique: Attention-UNet surrogate (uncertainty model) + multi-objective whale optimization algorithm (MOWOA)
core_claim: A composite reactive resonant shielding coil (nanocrystalline material + aluminum plates), designed via robust multi-objective optimization with an Attention-UNet surrogate model, can effectively shield the leakage electromagnetic field of an 11 kW EV-WPT system and ensure personnel exposure safety while maintaining high transfer efficiency even under uncertainty.
results: After shielding, the average induced electric field intensity in the body decreased by 48.38% (by 43.09% in the lungs), the average transfer efficiency increased by 6.46%, and the probability of exceeding the ICNIRP 2010 limit dropped from 43.09% to 0%; the surrogate model combined with MOWOA reduced the optimization computation time from 1958.3 h to 156.8 h.
limitations: Verification was performed only through simulation; the authors state that in the future they intend to investigate electromagnetic exposure safety mechanisms based on animal studies and to develop more cost-effective shielding structures.
source_file: Design of composite reactive resonance shielding coil for EV-WPT system based on hybrid robust optimization method.pdf

title: Surrounding Active Shielding Coil Design and Efficient Multiobjective Optimization for WPT
ai_technique: Data-driven polynomial-chaos metamodel + NSGA-II
core_claim: The proposed surrounding active shielding coil, connected in reverse series with the WPT coil, suppresses the leakage magnetic field of the WPT system without significant efficiency loss and can be rapidly designed using data-driven multi-objective optimization that combines a polynomial-chaos metamodel with the NSGA-II algorithm.
results: On a 300 W WPT system, the shielding coil reduced the leakage magnetic field by 86.97% and the induced electric field in the human body by 65.40%, while the transfer efficiency dropped by only 0.28%; the optimization time was reduced from 983.2 h to 432.4 s.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: verification was performed only on a laboratory 300 W system with a specific coil geometry; the metamodel is tied to the chosen design space, and transferability to other WPT configurations is not demonstrated.
source_file: Surrounding_Active_Shielding_Coil_Design_and_Efficient_Multiobjective_Optimization_for_WPT.pdf

title: Machine learning-assisted rapid electromagnetic design of flexible graphene-based absorptive composites
ai_technique: Random Forest regression within the DCPRO system for predicting dielectric properties and optimizing impedance matching
core_claim: The authors developed the DCPRO (Dielectric Constant Prediction and Reflection Optimization) system, which uses a machine learning model (Random Forest regression) to predict the electromagnetic properties of flexible graphene absorbing composites from key process parameters and subsequently designs a two-layer impedance-graded structure.
results: The designed two-layer impedance-graded structure achieved broadband absorption (EAB) in the range of 3.29-18 GHz with a minimum reflection loss (RLmin) of -56.08 dB; the approach expanded the prediction database and significantly shortened the research cycle compared to traditional trial-and-error methods.
limitations: Not stated explicitly in the available excerpt (the discussion/conclusion section is missing from the extracted text); this presumably concerns the dependence of prediction accuracy on the extent and quality of the training data for the specific graphene system.
source_file: old/253 - Machine learning-assisted rapid electromagnetic design of flexible graphene-based absorptive composites.pdf

title: Inverse Design of Ultra-Wideband Frequency Selective Surface Using a Graph Based Conditional Variational Autoencoder Integrated With a Physics Informed Neural Network
ai_technique: Graph-based conditional variational autoencoder (G-CVAE) + PINN
core_claim: Integrating a graph-based conditional variational autoencoder (G-CVAE) with a physics-informed neural network (PINN) enables the inverse design of an ultra-wideband bandstop FSS for EMI shielding that is both data-consistent and physically realizable, including angular and polarization stability.
results: The G-CVAE-PINN framework achieves an average error of approximately 3%; the fabricated and measured FSS exhibits a stopband of 8–28 GHz (20 GHz width), a shielding effectiveness of 80 dB, polarization independence, and angular stability up to 75°, with a compact 5×5 mm cell (0.08 λg).
limitations: Uncertainty quantification shows that predictions near resonant dips (sharp spectral notches) have reduced reliability and must be interpreted with caution; the authors attribute minor measurement deviations to model approximation and manufacturing tolerances.
source_file: Inverse_Design_of_Ultra-Wideband_Frequency_Selective_Surface_Using_a_Graph_Based_Conditional_Variational_Autoencoder_Integrated_With_a_Physics_Informed_Neural_Network.pdf

title: Machine Learning-Based Prediction of Shielding Effectiveness for Apertures in Irregular Cavities
ai_technique: Neural network (regression surrogate model for large-to-small aperture mapping)
core_claim: The authors propose an ML model that uses a neural network to map the response of a scaled-up aperture model onto the response of the actual small aperture, enabling fast prediction of the shielding effectiveness of irregular cavities without the need for fine meshing in a full EM simulation.
results: The trained neural network can reproduce the time-domain electric field waveforms inside the cavity with high accuracy (e.g., for an aperture width of 8 cm), in good agreement with the reference data; the authors do not report specific numerical errors (dB, %) in the abstract/introduction.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the method was verified only for a limited range of aperture sizes in a single cavity configuration (aircraft bay), and generalization to other geometries and materials was not discussed in the extracted text.
source_file: Machine_Learning-Based_Prediction_of_Shielding_Effectiveness_for_Apertures_in_Irregular_Cavities.pdf

title: Ensemble machine learning framework for predicting and optimizing electromagnetic shielding of Al 6061 hybrid metal matrix composites
ai_technique: Weighted average ensemble (WAE) of five ML models + feature importance, PDP/ICE
core_claim: A weighted-average ensemble of five ML algorithms can quickly and accurately predict the shielding effectiveness of Al 6061–SiC–fly ash hybrid composites and, together with feature-importance analysis, replace costly trial-and-error development of EMI shielding materials.
results: The ensemble model (WAE) achieved R² = 0.981 and outperformed the individual algorithms; frequency, transmission coefficients, and electrical conductivity were identified as the most important parameters; PDP/ICE analyses provided interpretable design recommendations for balancing dielectric and conductive losses.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the framework is limited to a single material system (Al 6061–SiC–fly ash), the dataset is not publicly available, and the predictions are not validated by an independent experiment outside the training data.
source_file: Ensemble machine learning framework for predicting and optimizing electromagnetic shielding of Al 6061 hybrid metal matrix composites.pdf

title: Machine Learning-Optimized High EMI Shielding Material Design for X-Band Satellite Electronics
ai_technique: Regression-based ML optimization of metasurface geometric parameters
core_claim: The authors present an ML-assisted design methodology for a compact metasurface EMI shielding structure for the X-band, in which a regression optimization framework efficiently fine-tunes the unit-cell geometric parameters.
results: The optimized structure achieves a shielding effectiveness of 57 dB at 10.2 GHz (S21 = -57 dB) with a subwavelength cell size of 3.2 mm, and stable broadband shielding over the 6-18 GHz range.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the work is based solely on simulations without experimental verification of a prototype; an analysis of the effect of manufacturing tolerances is missing.
source_file: Machine_Learning-Optimized_High_EMI_Shielding_Material_Design_for_X-Band_Satellite_Electronics.pdf

title: Machine Learning Approach for Predicting Electromagnetic Shielding Effectiveness of Metallic Grids in the Time and Frequency Domains
ai_technique: MLP neural network + NN surrogate optimization
core_claim: MLP neural networks trained on full-wave simulations (FIT/CST) can predict the shielding effectiveness of metal meshes in both the time domain (TDSE) and frequency domain (FDSE) from five structural and material parameters, and serve as a surrogate for fast design optimization against EMP-type transient events.
results: For TDSE prediction, the model achieved an RMSE of 0.02215 and R² = 0.9846; FDSE predictions in the 1–4 GHz band agree with CST with a maximum error below 1.1 dB; surrogate optimization found a design in 39 s versus 630 s for the Trust Region method in CST, at comparable accuracy.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the datasets originate exclusively from simulations without experimental validation; FDSE is covered only in the 1–4 GHz band, and the conclusions apply to the investigated type of metal mesh on a dielectric substrate.
source_file: IET Science Measure   Tech - 2026 - Kalantarnia - Machine Learning Approach for Predicting Electromagnetic Shielding.pdf

title: Machine Learning-Accelerated Discovery of High-Performance Insulating Electromagnetic Interference Shielding Composites for Integrated Electronics
ai_technique: ANN (combined with SHAP analysis and FEM simulations)
core_claim: The authors propose an ML framework (ANN) combined with finite-element simulations and SHAP analysis, which reveals that uniformly distributed fillers significantly increase the induced current in microcapacitors compared to the commonly used non-uniform fillers, leading to a design rule for electrically insulating EMI shielding composites.
results: The ANN model achieved the lowest MSE (6.2±0.1) and MRE (2.6±0.1%) compared to RF, DT, LR, EN, and SVR (MSE 81.7–98.4); a composite fabricated with uniform Ga particles based on the revealed rule achieved a shielding effectiveness above 90 dB in the Ka-band, a thermal conductivity of 3.7±0.1 W/m·K, and a resistivity of 1.7×10^12 Ω·m over the range −196 to 200 °C.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the work focuses on a single specific mechanism (microcapacitors) and a single filler type (Ga particles); the transferability of the derived rules to other material systems has not been experimentally verified.
source_file: Advanced Materials - 2025 - Liu - Machine Learning‐Accelerated Discovery of High‐Performance Insulating Electromagnetic.pdf

title: Development and Optimisational Study of Porous PVDF/Ag/BaTiO3/Gr Nanocomposite Foams for Effective Electromagnetic Interference Shielding Applications Using Statistical and Machine Learning Approaches
ai_technique: RSM (CCD) + Gradient Descent Algorithm (GDA) for SE prediction and optimization
core_claim: The paper combines a statistical method (RSM/CCD) with an optimization algorithm (Gradient Descent Algorithm, GDA) to predict shielding effectiveness (SE) and to find the optimal filler composition (Ag, BaTiO3, Gr) of a porous PVDF nanocomposite foam for EMI shielding in the X-band.
results: The best sample (2.5 g Ag, 2.5 g BaTiO3, 0.2 g Gr, porosity 95.6%, crystallinity 64.9%) achieved a maximum shielding effectiveness of 35.9 dB in the 8–12 GHz band; both RSM and GDA predictions agreed with the experiment with a mean absolute percentage error below 2%.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: in the "future work" section, the authors mention that the long-term durability, mechanical strength, and thermal stability of the foam under real operating conditions, as well as the scalability of the manufacturing process, have not been verified.
source_file: DEVELOPMENT AND OPTIMISATIONAL STUDY OF POROUS PVDF:Ag:BaTiO3:Gr NANOCOMPOSITE FOAMS FOR EFFECTIVE ELECTROMAGNETIC INTERFERENCE SHIELDING APPLICATIONS USING STATISTICAL AND MACHINE LEARNING APPROACHES.pdf

title: Machine Learning-Empowered Electromagnetic Wave Absorbing Materials: From Forward Prediction to Generative Inverse Design
ai_technique: Review: classical ML, deep learning, generative models (GAN/VAE/diffusion), PINN, LLM/AI agents
core_claim: This review article argues that machine learning is transforming research on electromagnetic absorbing materials from an empirical trial-and-error approach into data- and knowledge-driven design — ranging from forward property prediction to generative inverse design of absorbers.
results: It systematically reviews applications of classical ML, deep learning, generative and physics-informed models, LLMs, and AI agents in forward prediction, high-throughput screening, inverse design, EM parameter decomposition, explainability, multi-objective optimization, and data augmentation for absorbers; it presents physical limits (Rozanov, Snoek) as a framing context.
limitations: The authors identify the main challenges of the field as the lack of data standardization and the need for physics-guided learning, foundation models, autonomous laboratories, and validation at engineering scale.
source_file: Machine Learning-Empowered Electromagnetic Wave Absorbing Materials From Forward Prediction to Generative Inverse Design.pdf

title: Artificial Intelligence driven design optimization of the additive manufacturing parameters for making wireless charging magnetic shield
ai_technique: ANN surrogate models + multi-objective genetic algorithm (Pareto optimization)
core_claim: The study combines FE simulations with ANN surrogate models and a multi-objective genetic algorithm to optimize the process parameters of LPBF additive manufacturing (laser power, scanning speed, layer thickness, substrate temperature, hatch spacing, beam diameter) for producing magnetic shielding for EV wireless charging, with the goal of minimizing residual stress and deformation.
results: The ANN surrogate models achieved high correlation coefficients for predicting residual stress and deformation; the multi-objective GA found Pareto-optimal solutions, e.g., residual stress of 1412–1870 MPa and deformation of 0.55–0.57 mm depending on the scenario, and FE validation confirmed these values with deviations within practical tolerance (e.g., 1508–1730 MPa, 0.59 mm).
limitations: Not stated explicitly: finding feasible Pareto solutions required repeated manual adjustment of the design-variable bounds; verified only for a single alloy (AlSi10Mg) and a specific shield geometry.
source_file: Artificial Intelligence driven design optimization of the additive manufacturing parameters for making wireless charging magnetic shield.pdf

title: Parametric modeling and neural-network-based prediction of cylindrical–spherical-cap magnetic shielding devices
ai_technique: BPNN (backpropagation neural network) for effective permeability prediction
core_claim: The authors develop a fast parametric modeling and prediction framework for cylindrical-spherical magnetic shielding devices, in which a neural network (BPNN) predicts the effective permeability based on geometric parameters and replaces computationally expensive nonlinear finite-element simulations.
results: The model enables fast parametric scanning of effective permeability and was experimentally validated on both small and large magnetic shields; the cylindrical-spherical structure exhibits better magnetic-field homogeneity compared to a conventional rectangular configuration.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the exact numerical prediction error of the BPNN model is not given in the extracted text; the model focuses on effective permeability rather than the complete 3D field, and its transferability to materials other than permalloy was not discussed.
source_file: Parametric modeling and neural-network-based prediction of cylindrical–spherical-cap magnetic shielding devices.pdf

title: A Reinforcement Learning-Based Multiphysics Optimization of Composite Material Component With Slot-Structure in UAV System
ai_technique: TD3 (reinforcement learning) + ETF-PCNN (predictive CNN surrogate)
core_claim: The paper proposes a TD3 reinforcement learning algorithm (ETF-TD3) combined with a predictive convolutional network (ETF-PCNN) for multiphysics (electromagnetic-thermal-flow) optimization of the shielding effectiveness (SE) of slotted composite components in UAV systems.
results: On three types of composite components (UAV frame, two electronics enclosures), ETF-TD3 outperformed PSO and GA in average SE (SE-AUC 0.9019 vs. 0.7564 for PSO and 0.6862 for GA for the UAV frame) and was the only method to simultaneously meet the SE, thermal management, and aerodynamic requirements in all scenarios; on new slot shapes (ellipse, triangle) it achieved an accuracy above 90%.
limitations: Pretraining the ETF-TD3 agent requires approximately 100 hours of computation; the authors state that future work will focus on improving generalization to more complex systems and other types of composite materials.
source_file: A_Reinforcement_Learning-Based_Multiphysics_Optimization_of_Composite_Material_Component_With_Slot-Structure_in_UAV_System.pdf

title: Electromagnetic Shielding Effectiveness Prediction of Stacked Chassis Based on Snow Ablation Optimizer Algorithm
ai_technique: Snow Ablation Optimizer (SAO) + Robinson's equivalent model and BLT equations
core_claim: Combining Robinson's equivalent circuit model, BLT equations, and the metaheuristic Snow Ablation Optimizer (SAO) makes it possible to estimate the characteristic parameters of a stacked enclosure from a small number of simulation points and to predict SE at any position on the central axis, thereby overcoming the large errors of classical analytical approaches for complex cavities.
results: From five interior points simulated in CST, the equivalent-model parameters were estimated using SAO; the predicted SE curves along the axis agree well with the full-wave simulation, and the method identifies the optimal SE distribution along the frequency axis for placing sensitive equipment, at significantly lower computational cost.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the method is verified only through simulation for a single type of stacked enclosure and for a vertically polarized, normally incident plane wave; experimental validation is lacking.
source_file: Electromagnetic Shielding Effectiveness Prediction of Stacked, Chassis Based on Snow Ablation Optimizer Algorithm.pdf

title: Optimization Design of Active and Passive Hybrid Shielding for Electric Vehicle's Wireless Power Transfer System
ai_technique: Extreme Learning Machine (ELM) as a surrogate model + multi-objective optimization algorithm
core_claim: The authors propose a hybrid active-passive shielding structure (surrounding active coils + a ferrite-slotted passive layer) to suppress magnetic-field leakage in an electric-vehicle WPT system, and combine an extreme learning machine (ELM) surrogate model with multi-objective optimization for the shielding structure design.
results: According to numerical simulations, the proposed solution simultaneously maintains the system's transfer efficiency and reduces magnetic leakage in all directions, thereby improving electromagnetic exposure safety for the human body; specific numerical SE/efficiency values are not given in the extracted text.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the work is based solely on numerical simulation without an experimental prototype; the ELM surrogate model was trained on a limited set of configurations.
source_file: Optimization Design of Active and Passive Hybrid Shielding, for Electric Vehicle’s Wireless Power Transfer System.pdf

title: Modeling and Optimization of EMI Filter by Using Artificial Neural Network
ai_technique: Partially connected ANN + GA
core_claim: The proposed partially connected ANN enables accurate and fast modeling of the insertion loss of an EMI filter, including high-frequency coupling, without the need for parasitic parameter extraction, and, in conjunction with a genetic algorithm, enables efficient optimization of the filter component selection.
results: The simulated and measured insertion loss of the optimized filter agree within the 100 kHz-108 MHz band with a maximum error of 6.8 dB (at 441 kHz); the GA optimization over the ANN model takes only a few minutes, and the resulting filter meets the requirement with a margin of over 6 dB across the entire band.
limitations: The authors report an increase in training computational cost with the number of filter parameters considered, a risk of ANN overfitting for an unsuitable choice of training parameters, and validation only on a common-mode filter; DM filters with more pronounced coupling require further research.
source_file: old/269 - Modeling and Optimization of EMI Filter by Using Artificial Neural Network.pdf

title: An Equivalent Dipole Model Hybrid With Artificial Neural Network for Electromagnetic Interference Prediction
ai_technique: ANN + equivalent dipole model (near-field scan)
core_claim: A hybrid equivalent-dipole model with an ANN can automatically account for multiple reflections and diffraction from surrounding PCB components -- which the traditional dipole model with a free-space Green's function neglects -- when reconstructing an EMI source from a near-field scan, thereby improving the accuracy of the radiated EMI field prediction.
results: The method was validated by both a numerical example and measurement of a patch antenna surrounded by four heat sinks; after training on the scanned near field, the ANN predicted the EMI field in the region of interest in agreement with the reference and better than the traditional dipole model (without providing a summary numerical metric).
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors attribute differences between measurement and simulation to probe positioning errors and uncertainty in substrate permittivity; the model requires a near-field scan and retraining for each specific board layout.
source_file: old/261 - An Equivalent Dipole Model Hybrid With Artificial Neural Network for Electromagnetic Interference Prediction.pdf

title: Machine-Learning-Based Hybrid Random-Fuzzy Uncertainty Quantification for EMC and SI Assessment
ai_technique: Bayesian optimization (GP regression) + polynomial chaos as a surrogate
core_claim: The authors propose a hybrid approach combining evidence theory, Bayesian optimization, and polynomial chaos for efficient quantification of combined aleatory and epistemic uncertainty in EMC and signal integrity problems, as a replacement for computationally expensive Monte Carlo/grid-search simulations.
results: The method was verified on two examples (radiated immunity of a twisted-wire pair and crosstalk of a bent differential microstrip) and provided accuracy comparable to the original hybrid MC/GS algorithm with a significantly lower number of required model evaluations (lower computational time according to the cost table).
limitations: The authors did not provide examples with a higher number of stochastic dimensions, since comparison with the reference MC/GS method would become computationally infeasible in that case.
source_file: old/78 - Machine-Learning-Based Hybrid Random-Fuzzy Uncertainty Quantification for EMC and SI Assessment.pdf

title: Prediction of Capacitor's Accelerated Aging Based on Advanced Measurements and Deep Neural Network Techniques
ai_technique: Conditional deep neural network (DNN) with dropout
core_claim: The authors propose a conditional deep neural network with dropout for predicting the accelerated aging behavior of capacitors (capacitance, ESR) that are key to EMC compliance (EMI/RFI filtering), based on data from a new continuous measurement method.
results: Thanks to DNN predictions, the measurement time can be shortened from a typical 1000 h to approximately 200 h or less while maintaining high prediction accuracy (high R² values in experimental validation); the method was verified on two types of film capacitors (PET and PP) under thermal and electrical stress.
limitations: The authors state that prediction accuracy decreases when shorter input measurement sequences are used (fewer measured points), and that the method was verified only on two types of capacitors under specific accelerated aging conditions.
source_file: old/83 - Prediction of Capacitor’s Accelerated Aging Based on Advanced Measurements and Deep Neural Network Techniques.pdf

title: Deep learning for both broadband prediction of the radiated emission from heatsinks and heatsink optimization
ai_technique: DNN + Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO)
core_claim: A deep learning model predicts the resonant frequencies and maximum radiated emissions (EMI) of plate-fin heat sinks in the 2-8 GHz band from geometric parameters, complemented by a Grey Wolf Optimizer (GWO) to find the optimal heat sink geometry with lower EMI.
results: R² reaches values of 0.96 / 0.98 / 0.97 / 0.99 for the quantities f1, f2, REmax1, REmax2; the average prediction deviation is 0.6% relative to simulation and 1.3% relative to measurement, on a dataset of 3888 modeled heat sinks.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the model is limited to the parametric space of plate-fin heat sinks in the 2-8 GHz band; validation against real measurements was performed on only two physical heat sinks.
source_file: old/227 - Deep learning for both broadband prediction of the radiated emission from heatsinks and heatsink optimization.pdf

title: A Deep Learning Based Macro Circuit Modeling for Black-box EMC Problems
ai_technique: Physics-informed fully connected DNN for the inverse circuit problem
core_claim: A physics-informed deep neural network enables deriving a low-order macro-circuit (PEEC) model of an unknown black-box device from measured near-field data for solving emission and immunity EMC problems.
results: On a proof-of-concept example (10x10 grid, 1000 samples), the DNN achieved good agreement between the predicted node potentials and the true PEEC model values, while a strategy using the magnitude and phase of the displacement current as input reduced the number of input neurons by nearly 50%.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: validation was carried out only on a simplified lossless PEEC model with a randomly generated trace; real multilayer and lossy structures were not tested.
source_file: old/60 - A Deep Learning Based Macro Circuit Modeling for Black-box EMC Problems.pdf

title: Machine Learning Regression Techniques for the Modeling of Complex Systems: An Overview
ai_technique: SVM, LS-SVM, and Gaussian Process Regression (surrogate models)
core_claim: The paper examines the effectiveness of advanced ML regressions (SVM, LS-SVM, Gaussian Process Regression) for building accurate and rapidly evaluable surrogate models that predict output quantities of EMC systems (e.g., a converter's emission spectral envelope) as a function of system parameters.
results: Surrogate models trained on a limited number of samples (L=50-300) accurately reproduce the results of a 10,000-sample Monte Carlo simulation; training takes 200 s for LS-SVM, 11 s for GPR, and 20 s for 2nd-order PCE, with GPR providing 95% confidence intervals that conservatively cover 85.5% of the tested current spectra.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the paper is conceived as a review/tutorial study using two illustrative examples; the general validity of the conclusions for a broader class of EMC problems has not been systematically verified.
source_file: Machine_Learning_Regression_Techniques_for_the_Modeling_of_Complex_Systems_An_Overview.pdf

title: A Neuro-Space Mapping Method for Harmonic Interference Prediction of SOIFET Radio Frequency Switches
ai_technique: Dynamic neuro-space mapping with LSTM (+ INC equivalent-circuit coarse model)
core_claim: A dynamic neuro-space mapping model that combines a simplified nonlinear capacitive (INC) coarse model with LSTM mapping networks can accurately and quickly predict harmonic interference of SOIFET RF switches, providing guidance for preventing EMI issues already at the RF front-end design stage.
results: The harmonic power prediction achieves a relative error of up to 6.44%; the prediction errors for all three monitored harmonics are below 7% and agree with measurements; the method is significantly more accurate than both static neuro-SM and the INC model alone.
limitations: The authors state that the model has not yet been generalized to other types of SOIFET switches; they leave the development of a generalizable harmonic-prediction model for different switch types to future work.
source_file: old/157 - A Neuro-Space Mapping Method for Harmonic Interference Prediction of SOIFET Radio Frequency Switches.pdf

title: Application of Non-Embedded Uncertainty Analysis Methods in Worst Case Estimation of the EMC
ai_technique: Kriging surrogate model (GPR) compared with Monte Carlo, stochastic collocation, and SROM
core_claim: The article compares four non-embedded uncertainty analysis methods (Monte Carlo, stochastic collocation, stochastic reduced-order model, and a Kriging surrogate model) for estimating worst-case scenarios in EMC problems, such as cable-bundle crosstalk and shielding effectiveness.
results: The Kriging surrogate model requires only 49-81 deterministic simulations compared to 80,000 for Monte Carlo, but for problems with higher nonlinearity its accuracy is substantially worse (e.g., an error of 43.4-69.2 versus the correct value of 1.032); stochastic collocation offers the best trade-off between accuracy and efficiency.
limitations: The study used only a two-dimensional model of random variables, so the dimension disaster problem for stochastic collocation did not manifest; for higher-dimensional problems, the authors recommend caution and improving the nonlinear capability of the Kriging model.
source_file: old/54 - Application of non-embedded uncertainty analysis methods in worst case estimation of the emc.pdf

title: Metaheuristic aided structural topology optimization method for heat sink design with low electromagnetic interference
ai_technique: MASTO (YUKI metaheuristic) + FEM
core_claim: The MASTO (metaheuristic-assisted structural topology optimization) method can, in a multiphysics problem, design a heat sink that simultaneously maximizes heat dissipation and minimizes EMI by suppressing the resonant (antenna-like) properties of the finned structure already at the design stage.
results: Optimized 2D heat sink topologies balancing thermal conductivity (including convection) and EMI suppression -- evaluated via impedance mismatch -- were obtained; the designs were fabricated and validated by measurement (VSWR, thermal measurements) in agreement with the simulations, without a summary numerical metric for EMI reduction.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the study is limited to 2D arrangements with elongated fins and evaluation of EMI via impedance matching to a known interference frequency; general 3D geometries and broadband excitation are not addressed.
source_file: old/265 - Metaheuristic aided structural topology optimization method for heat sink design with low electromagnetic interference.pdf

title: Deep learning method for predicting electromagnetic emission spectrum of aerospace equipment
ai_technique: LSTM + Bayesian hyperparameter optimization
core_claim: The authors propose a deep learning method (an LSTM network with Bayesian hyperparameter optimization) combined with threshold-based data decomposition for predicting the electromagnetic emission spectrum of spacecraft equipment in EMC tests.
results: On three examples (conducted emission of a signal line, conducted emission of a power line, radiated electric-field emission), the predicted emission spectrum matched the test data at a 95% confidence level.
limitations: The authors note a trade-off between the decomposition threshold setting and computational cost: a lower threshold increases prediction accuracy but requires more computational resources and a longer computation time.
source_file: old/53 - Deep learning method for predicting electromagnetic emission spectrum of aerospace equipment.pdf

title: Machine learning based electric field budgeting methodology for EMC critical systems
ai_technique: 3D CNN (U-Net) as a surrogate model for EM simulation
core_claim: A surrogate model based on a 3D CNN (U-Net), trained on data from a 3D electromagnetic simulator (CST), predicts the spatial distribution of the electric field within a system from the emissions of individual units and the 3D geometry, enabling fast determination of radiated emission limits (electric field budgeting) for EMC-critical systems, e.g., spacecraft.
results: The trained model predicts new 3D field maps with an MAE error < 3 dB per scene/frequency on the order of milliseconds, compared to the 15-20 minutes (simple geometry) to tens of hours (complex geometry) required for a full EM simulation; it enables thousands of Monte Carlo runs to be performed within a single hour.
limitations: The model is accurate only within the range of parameters and geometries used during training; it requires extensive simulation-based training data and further fine-tuning on real measurements, which has not yet been performed in this work.
source_file: old/234 - Machine Learning Based Electric Field Budgeting Methodology for EMC Critical Systems.pdf

title: PGPR-GA hybrid framework for automated EMI filter design
ai_technique: Piecewise Gaussian Process Regression (PGPR) surrogate + genetic algorithm (GA)
core_claim: The authors propose an automated optimization framework for EMI filter design that combines a piecewise Gaussian process regression (PGPR) surrogate model, capturing the filter's frequency-dependent behavior, with a genetic algorithm (GA) to find an optimal, commercially available (BOM-ready) filter configuration.
results: The PGPR surrogate achieves a coefficient of determination of R²=0.99 when validated on 75 filter configurations, with an uncertainty coverage of 98.3%; the autonomously designed and manufactured filter met the CISPR 32 Class B standard, with a prediction agreement of RMSE = 0.62 dB.
limitations: The authors state that the framework is currently limited to a single filter topology (Π-type CLC), and recommend future extension with transfer learning for more complex topologies, full multi-objective optimization, and real-time adaptive design using reinforcement learning.
source_file: PGPR-GA hybrid framework for automated EMI filter design.pdf

title: An AI-Based Pareto-Driven Cost-Dimension Optimization of EMI filters for DC-DC Converters
ai_technique: MLP feasibility-range classifier + Pareto-dominance optimization
core_claim: The paper proposes an AI-assisted Pareto optimization algorithm for the synthesis of passive EMI filters for DC-DC converters, which minimizes cost and footprint/volume while ensuring the required EMI attenuation and system stability, using real catalogs of commercial components.
results: On two commercial boards (TI LM25149-Q1EVM-2100, ST STEVAL-L3751V12), the algorithm found solutions that outperformed the reference designs in both cost and footprint/volume simultaneously; an auxiliary MLP classifier for estimating computational cost achieved an accuracy of 98.7%.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the method was verified only on two specific buck-converter boards and a component dataset from a single distributor (Mouser); generality for other converter topologies was not tested.
source_file: An_AI-Based_Pareto-Driven_Cost-Dimension_Optimization_of_EMI_filters_for_DC-DC_Converters.pdf

title: Deep Surrogate Modeling for Conducted EMI Prediction and Filter Optimization in a Three-Level NPC Inverter: From Experimental Data to Compliance-Aware Design
ai_technique: ANN/CNN/DNN/PINN surrogate models for conducted EMI prediction
core_claim: The study proposes an experimentally grounded framework with deep surrogate models (ANN, CNN, DNN, PINN) for predicting conducted EMI in a three-phase NPC converter with an active harmonic filter, and their use in optimizing a filter that meets regulatory emission limits.
results: CNN achieved the most consistent and stable predictive performance among the models; surrogate-driven optimization improved the worst-case regulatory emission margin by 15.20 dBµV (from −3.7 dBµV before optimization to 7.26–11.50 dBµV for the verified optimized designs).
limitations: The authors state that a low aggregate model error does not guarantee physically plausible spectral behavior, which was particularly evident in the "standby peak" scenario, where the spectral structure is difficult to learn.
source_file: Deep Surrogate Modeling for Conducted EMI Prediction and Filter Optimization in a Three-Level NPC Inverter From Experimental Data to Compliance-Aware Design.pdf

title: Automated Near-Field Scanning Algorithm for the EMC Analysis of Electronic Devices
ai_technique: Kriging surrogate model + sequential sampling
core_claim: The authors propose an automated sequential sampling algorithm with a Kriging surrogate model that minimizes the number of measurement points needed to characterize the electromagnetic near field of electronic devices for EMC analysis purposes.
results: The method was successfully verified on both simulated and measured printed circuit board examples, where it achieved highly accurate reconstruction of the near-field zone with a substantially lower number of scan points than conventional uniform sampling.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the method was demonstrated only on a limited number of PCB examples; behavior for more complex 3D geometries and higher frequencies was not systematically examined.
source_file: old/4 -Automated Near-Field Scanning Algorithm for the EMC Analysis of Electronic Devices.pdf

title: Adaptive Classification Algorithm for EMC-Compliance Testing of Electronic Devices
ai_technique: LS-SVM/ANN classification + sequential sampling
core_claim: The authors present an adaptive classification algorithm that, using sequential sampling and a classification model (LS-SVM/ANN), identifies regions of low/medium/high near-field amplitude during EMC compliance testing with a minimal number of measurements.
results: The method was verified on a measured microstrip line bend, where it enabled accurate delineation of the three interference region classes with a substantially lower number of measurements than a full device scan; the specific percentage of measurement savings is not given in the available text.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: validation was carried out on only a single test sample (microstrip bend); the general robustness of the method for more complex devices has not been demonstrated.
source_file: old/5 -Adaptive classification algorithm for EMC-compliance testing of electronic devices.pdf

title: Optimized Planar Elliptical Dipole Antenna for UWB EMC Applications
ai_technique: Invasive Weed Optimization (IWO) + PSO + DE
core_claim: The authors propose a planar elliptical dipole antenna for UWB EMC measurements, optimized using the invasive weed optimization (IWO) metaheuristic algorithm and its modified version, compared against PSO and DE.
results: The modified IWO achieved better results than conventional IWO, PSO, and DE in optimizing the antenna's return loss, realized gain, and gain flatness in the 1-5 GHz band, thereby demonstrating superiority over the other methods.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the optimization was verified only through simulation (CST) for one antenna type; the performance of the modified IWO on other antenna structures was not tested.
source_file: old/50 - Optimized Planar Elliptical Dipole Antenna for UWB EMC Applications.pdf

title: Estimating Mean of Maximum Fields Inside Reverberation Chambers Using Deep Neural Networks
ai_technique: Deep feedforward neural network (MLP)
core_claim: A deep feedforward neural network (regression model) predicts the mean value of the maximum field inside a nested reverberation chamber (whose distribution deviates from the Rayleigh distribution) as a function of frequency, as an alternative to the traditional estimation using maximum-likelihood estimation of GEV distribution parameters.
results: The network with five hidden layers achieved a training/test mean squared error (MSE) of 325e-5 / 625e-5 and provides results approximately ten times faster than the traditional MLE method, without the need for retraining for new frequency steps.
limitations: The authors state that the network's performance could be further improved by using an ensemble of networks and Monte Carlo cross-validation, which they leave for future work; the method was validated for only one aperture configuration and would need to be extended to other aperture shapes and measurement positions.
source_file: old/237 - Estimating Mean of Maximum Fields Inside Reverberation Chambers Using Deep Neural Networks.pdf

title: Improving antenna gain estimations in non-ideal test sites with auto-tunable filters
ai_technique: PSO (particle swarm optimization) for tuning adaptive filter parameters and the time-gating window
core_claim: The authors propose a computational tool with automatically tunable adaptive filters optimized using PSO, which improves antenna gain estimation in non-ideal open-area test sites (OATS) used for EMC antenna calibration, without requiring the operator to have signal-processing expertise.
results: The new fitness function for PSO filter optimization achieved a higher average correlation between the filtered and reference (reflection-free) gain curve (0.86 versus 0.81 for the classical MSE fitness), and the method outperformed the traditional time-gating approach, especially at low signal-to-noise ratio, requiring fewer measurements.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: validation was performed only on a limited number of antennas and two specific measurement facilities (UFMG, IFSTTAR); generalizability to other types of environments, antennas, and frequency bands was not verified.
source_file: old/126 - Improving antenna gain estimations in non-ideal test sites with auto-tunable filters.pdf

title: Estimation of electromagnetic far-field from near-field using machine learning
ai_technique: XGBoost (Gradient Boosting Decision Tree)
core_claim: A machine learning model (GBDT/XGBoost) estimates the far-field radiated emissions of a printed circuit board from a near-field scan, which could replace costly anechoic chamber measurements in EMC testing.
results: The RMSPE of the far-field intensity estimate remained consistently below 7.1% in the 1-6 GHz band, with the best value of approximately 4.5% at 1 GHz, based on 2000 training and 500 test samples.
limitations: The dataset was generated purely by simulation (FDTD method) for a single substrate size and randomly placed conductive traces; the method has not been validated against real measurements.
source_file: old/222 - Estimation of electromagnetic far-field from near-field using machine learning.pdf

title: Near-Field Prediction in Complex Environment Based on Phaseless Scanned Fields and Machine Learning
ai_technique: ANN with Bayesian regularization, Green's function input, phaseless scanned near field
core_claim: The authors propose a method based on an artificial neural network (ANN) with Green's functions as input, which predicts the radiated field of an unknown EMI source in a complex environment with multiple reflections from a phaseless (magnitude-only) scanned near field, without the need to measure the field phase.
results: The method was validated by both numerical and measurement experiments (an antenna between two parallel metal plates) and achieved higher accuracy and robustness in predicting the field magnitude on validation planes (2.353 GHz and 3.724 GHz) than the traditional interpolation method and the differential evolution (DE) method.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors merely note better accuracy compared to the benchmark methods without discussing specific limitations; this presumably concerns the need to predefine the number and position of equivalent dipoles according to the DUT geometry and the scanned field.
source_file: old/238 - Near-Field Prediction in Complex Environment Based on Phaseless Scanned Fields and Machine Learning.pdf

title: Experimental Prediction of the Radiated Emission and Final Measurement Process Optimization based on Deep Neural Networks According to EN 55032
ai_technique: 1D CNN and hybrid CNN-LSTM network for predicting the worst-case position in radiated EMI measurement
core_claim: The authors propose predicting the position of maximum radiated emission (turntable azimuth and antenna height) during the final phase of EMC measurement per the EN 55032 standard using a 1D CNN and a hybrid CNN-LSTM architecture, thereby shortening the measurement time.
results: The model achieved savings of up to 75% in turntable rotation time and 83.29% in antenna height positioning time, at an acceptable position prediction error (on the order of 13-17%); the total duration of the final measurement phase was reduced by up to 40% compared to the R&S EMC32 software.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the authors only mention the need for real training data from the Cetecom chamber for specific EUT types; this presumably concerns limited generalization to other device types and frequency ranges outside the trained set.
source_file: old/243 - Experimental Prediction of the Radiated Emission and Final Measurement Process Optimization based on Deep Neural Networks According to EN 55032.pdf

title: Development and Evaluation of a CNN-LSTM Architecture based Neural Network for Time Optimization during EMI Measurements
ai_technique: 1D CNN and hybrid CNN-LSTM network for predicting the worst-case EUT position in radiated EMI measurement
core_claim: Similarly to a companion paper by the same authors (per the 47 CFR §15.209 standard instead of EN 55032), the authors propose predicting the worst-case azimuth and antenna height using a 1D CNN and a hybrid CNN-LSTM network to shorten the final phase of EMI measurement.
results: At an acceptable position error (16.78% for 1D-CNN, 8.21% for CNN-LSTM), up to 75% of the turntable rotation time can be saved, and at an antenna height error of 34.03%/19.11%, up to 83.29% of the height positioning time can be saved; overall, up to 50% of the final measurement phase duration can be saved compared to the R&S EMC32 software.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: similarly to the companion paper, the authors do not state explicit limitations; this presumably concerns dependence on the size and representativeness of the training set of measurements from a specific chamber, and limited transferability to other devices.
source_file: old/244 - Development and Evaluation of a CNN-LSTM Architecture based Neural Network for Time Optimization during EMI Measurements.pdf

title: AI Assisted Interference Classification to Improve EMC Troubleshooting in Electronic System Development
ai_technique: Deep neural network (DNN) + PCA for dimensionality reduction
core_claim: The authors use a deep neural network to classify different PCB designs based on their simulated electromagnetic frequency spectra obtained from near-field-to-far-field transformation, in order to identify the probable cause of EMC limit exceedance.
results: The best model (4 layers, 128 neurons, 100 input features after PCA) achieved 99.88% accuracy, 99.89% precision, 99.86% recall, and 99.88% F1 score on test data from 22 PCB design classes.
limitations: The authors state that the model was trained only on synthetic (simulated) data and needs to be verified on real measurements of manufactured PCBs; furthermore, multiple parameters were varied simultaneously in the experiment, which complicates unambiguous identification of the error cause.
source_file: old/86 - AI Assisted Interference Classification to Improve EMC Troubleshooting in Electronic System Development.pdf

title: Field Deviation in Radiated Emission Measurement in Anechoic Chamber for Frequencies up to 60 GHz and Deep Learning-Based Correction
ai_technique: CNN + transformer (hybrid deep learning)
core_claim: A hybrid deep learning model (CNN + transformer) corrects the radiated emission (RE) measurement deviation in an anechoic chamber caused by the mismatch between the receiver's vertical scanning plane and the actual wavefront, at frequencies up to 60 GHz.
results: The average correction error is 6.35% for a 3 m distance in a semi-anechoic chamber and less than 4.83% for the other tested scenarios.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the method was validated only for specific chamber geometries and measurement distances up to 60 GHz, and it depends on a large training dataset from simulations/measurements.
source_file: old/220 - Field Deviation in Radiated Emission Measurement in AnechoicChamber for Frequencies up to 60 GHz and Deep Learning-Based Correction.pdf

title: Predict and Eliminate EMI Signals for RF Shielding-Free MRI via Simultaneous Sensing and Deep Learning
ai_technique: 5-layer CNN (convolution + batch normalization + ReLU) for predicting the EMI signal from reference sensing coils
core_claim: The authors propose a method combining simultaneous EMI sensing with reference coils and deep learning (CNN), which predicts and removes the EMI signal from the received MRI data, thereby enabling MRI scanner operation without an RF-shielded room.
results: The method reliably removed EMI from various external and internal sources on both a 0.055 T and a 1.5 T MRI scanner; the resulting image SNR was comparable (within about 5%) to images acquired in a fully shielded room.
limitations: Not stated explicitly: the short conference paper does not include a separate limitations section; this presumably concerns the need to train the CNN model for each measurement/coil configuration, and validation so far limited to only two scanner types.
source_file: old/240 - Predict and Eliminate EMI Signals for RF Shielding-Free MRI via Simultaneous Sensing and Deep Learning.pdf

title: Machine Learning Based Classification and Prediction of Electromagnetic Absorption in Electrical Reverberation Chambers
ai_technique: Autoencoder (dimensionality reduction) + CNN classification
core_claim: A method combining an autoencoder (for S-parameter dimensionality reduction) with machine learning classification models is intended to rapidly classify devices by their susceptibility to absorbing power from the surrounding EM field in an electromagnetic reverberation chamber, which could be used for fast EMC immunity/shielding testing.
results: For four generic devices under test (monopoles with a known resonant frequency of 1.25-2 GHz), the resonant frequency was identified by fitting a Lorentzian model with a deviation of 3.0-4.3% (R² 27.5-53.9%); the ML classification via the autoencoder itself is described conceptually, with the author deferring comprehensive quantitative results to planned future measurements.
limitations: Low signal-to-noise ratio (approximately 0 dB) in the raw reverberation chamber data; the method has so far been validated only on a small number of generic samples, and the full ML classification results are not yet presented in this conference paper.
source_file: old/233 - Machine Learning Based Classification and Prediction of Electromagnetic Absorption in Electrical Reverberation Chambers.pdf

title: Evaluation of Simulated and Real Measurement Data for AI-based Interference Classification in EMC Applications
ai_technique: Autoencoder (AE) for feature extraction + Pearson correlation analysis
core_claim: The study compares real and simulated PCB emission measurements using an autoencoder for dimensionality reduction and Pearson correlation between features and output classes, in order to assess the accuracy and transferability of simulated data for training an AI classifier of EMC problem causes.
results: Real data showed high correlation of features with the output classes frequency and conductor length (r up to 0.9), but low correlation for line impedance, termination, and decaps; no significant correlation (r < 0.3) was found between features of the simulated data and the output classes of the real data, indicating limited transferability of simulated data to real measurements.
limitations: The authors state that some output classes require additional data transformation due to differing scale and dimensionality between real and simulated data, which limits direct transferability between simulation and reality.
source_file: old/85 - Evaluation of Simulated and Real Measurement Data for AI-based Interference Classification in EMC Applications.pdf

title: AI-Assisted Identification of State and Type of Flat-Panel Monitors in the Presence of EM Noise
ai_technique: Ensemble of MLPs (ANN) + automatic selection of significant frequency sub-bands
core_claim: An ensemble of independently trained MLP neural networks can identify the type and state (on/off) of flat-panel monitors from EM emanations measured in a real noisy environment; the proposed frequency sub-band significance analysis additionally automatically selects relevant inputs and simplifies the networks.
results: The method identifies the type and state of the monitor from a set of 12 units with 99% accuracy; the sub-band significance analysis reduced the number of network inputs 7-fold without loss of accuracy and shortened the average training time by nearly 30% (training took about 690 s on a standard PC).
